message.content: My frontend container keeps getting `curl: (6) Could not resolve host: backend` when it tries to call the backend. Both containers are up according to `docker compose ps`. What's going on?

content.content:
version: '3.9'

services:
  backend:
    build:
      context: ./backend
      dockerfile: Dockerfile
    ports:
      - '8000:8000'
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:secret@db:5432/appdb
    networks:
      - app-network
    depends_on:
      - db

  frontend:
    build:
      context: ./frontend
      dockerfile: Dockerfile
    ports:
      - '3000:3000'
    environment:
      - REACT_APP_API_URL=http://backend:8000
    depends_on:
      - backend

  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_USER=postgres
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret
      - POSTGRES_DB=appdb
    volumes:
      - db-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    networks:
      - app-network

networks:
  app-network:
    driver: bridge

volumes:
  db-data:

input.old_string:
frontend:
    build:
      context: ./frontend
      dockerfile: Dockerfile
    ports:
      - '3000:3000'
    environment:
      - REACT_APP_API_URL=http://backend:8000
    depends_on:
      - backend
input.new_string:
frontend:
    build:
      context: ./frontend
      dockerfile: Dockerfile
    ports:
      - '3000:3000'
    environment:
      - REACT_APP_API_URL=http://backend:8000
    networks:
      - app-network
    depends_on:
      - backend

content.text:
Found the problem. Your `frontend` service had no `networks` key, so Docker attached it to the default bridge network rather than `app-network`. The `backend` and `db` services were on `app-network`, which has its own internal DNS — that's why `backend` hostname wasn't resolvable from the frontend container.

I've added `networks: - app-network` to the `frontend` service. Now all three services share the same network and can reach each other by service name.

Recreate the containers to apply the change:
```bash
docker compose up -d --force-recreate frontend
```

You can verify all containers are on the same network with:

input.description: List the app-network to show its ID and driver

content.content:
NETWORK ID     NAME                   DRIVER
a3f9b1c2d4e5   webapp_app-network     bridge

content.text:
The network `webapp_app-network` exists and is using the bridge driver as expected. After you recreate the frontend container, you can confirm all three services are attached to it with:

```bash
docker network inspect webapp_app-network --format '{{range .Containers}}{{.Name}} {{end}}'
```

You should see `webapp-frontend-1`, `webapp-backend-1`, and `webapp-db-1` all listed. At that point `http://backend:8000` will resolve correctly from inside the frontend container.

message.content: That fixed it! All three containers show up in the network inspect output now. I can't believe such a small thing caused that — I should have caught it in review.

content.text:
Easy to miss — Docker silently puts services with no `networks` key on a default network, and everything looks fine in `docker compose ps` because the containers are running. The connectivity failure only shows up at runtime.

A good way to catch this in future reviews: if any service defines explicit `networks`, do a quick scan to make sure every service that needs to talk to another is in a shared network. You could also add a simple smoke-test step to your CI that curls between containers after `docker compose up`, which would surface this kind of issue automatically before it reaches review.

lastPrompt: That fixed it! All three containers show up in the network inspect output now. I can't believe such a small thing caused that — I should have caught it in review.
